network.overall_strategy: This network centers valine formation on the literature-supported Strecker route from isobutyraldehyde, ammonia, and hydrogen cyanide, with hydrolysis of the valine aminonitrile as the primary target-forming step. To respect the research findings, the network explicitly treats branched-precursor generation as the main bottleneck and therefore uses low-selectivity aldol diversification of acetaldehyde to branched aldehydes as a speculative but necessary connector rather than overstating selective synthesis. A second, metabolically congruent branch proceeds through alpha-ketoisovalerate and mineral-mediated reductive amination under moderate alkaline hydrothermal conditions. Surface chemistry supplies HCN-rich and concentration-favoring settings for Strecker, Bucherer-Bergs, aminonitrile protection, and hydrolysis, while hydrothermal settings provide reduced carbon, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and mineral redox catalysis. Biochemical/prebiotic settings complete hydrolysis, transamination-style network closure, and peptide incorporation. Ten distinct pathways are defined by different combinations of these validated and auxiliary branches.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde and acetaldehyde inventory

conditions.other: anoxic hydrothermal setting; Fischer-Tropsch-type low-selectivity reduced carbon generation

reactions.mechanism: CO2 hydrogenation on Fe-bearing mineral surfaces gives a low-selectivity reduced carbon mixture including C1 aldehydes and C2 oxygenates that can seed downstream chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe/Ni-type Fischer-Tropsch chemistry is supported for production of hydrocarbons, alcohols, and oxygenates rather than direct amino acids, and is most appropriate here as a source of aldehyde inventory feeding later Strecker chemistry (McCollom et al., 1999). This step is intentionally low-selectivity and avoids overstating direct isobutyraldehyde formation; it also reflects the hydrothermal reduced-carbon supply emphasized in alkaline vent scenarios (Amend & Shock, 1998; Martin & Russell, 2007).

reactions.name: Surface HCN and formamide generation from atmospheric discharge and photochemical feedstocks

conditions.other: UV-irradiated evaporitic or volcanic pool receiving atmospheric/discharge products; product concentration during drying

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric/discharge chemistry and UV processing generate cyanide-bearing species and formamide-containing inventories that accumulate in surface pools; TiO2 supports associated photocatalytic carbon-nitrogen chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature does not support a clean single-step NH3-to-HCN conversion, so this node is framed as a surface concentration and transformation of photochemical/discharge feedstocks rather than a falsely specific stoichiometric synthesis. HCN is a central reagent in Miller-Urey style chemistry and cyanide-rich surface networks, while formamide is a documented UV-accessible downstream reservoir in prebiotic settings (Miller, 1953; Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Saladino et al., 2006). This reaction is therefore a necessary connector and explicitly acknowledged as a pooled environmental source term grounded in the discharge/photochemical literature.

reactions.name: NiS-mediated aldol diversification to branched C4 aldehydes including isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling; low-selectivity aldol network; branched products expected at low abundance

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde undergoes aldol diversification and rearrangement in a complex network on sulfide-rich mineral surfaces, producing higher aldehydes including branched C4 species such as isobutyraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This step directly addresses the main literature bottleneck and is deliberately written as low-selectivity diversification, not a clean one-step selective synthesis. Branched aldehydes are reported among diversified acetaldehyde-derived products in NiS-catalyzed acetylene/aldehyde chemistry, but selective isobutyraldehyde synthesis remains unresolved (Preiner et al., 2023). The research report explicitly states that aldol condensation can furnish branched C3-C4 species but with poor selectivity; this reaction preserves that limitation rather than overstating confidence.

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker formation of valine aminonitrile

conditions.other: pH approximately 9; free NH3 available; mild aqueous evaporitic pool conditions

reactions.mechanism: Isobutyraldehyde reacts with ammonia to form an imine, followed by cyanide addition to yield the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of valine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best-supported prebiotic route to valine. The literature specifically identifies isobutyraldehyde as the Strecker aldehyde precursor for valine and supports aminonitrile formation under mild alkaline aqueous conditions near pH 9 (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Keefe et al., 1995; Devergne et al., 2021). Clay/mineral concentration is included to fit the required surface-mineral framework without altering the validated core mechanism.

reactions.name: Formamide-mediated N-formyl protection of valine aminonitrile

conditions.other: formamide-rich drying films or concentrated pools; protective derivatization of aminonitrile

reactions.mechanism: The aminonitrile undergoes N-formylation in formamide-rich media, suppressing reversal of the Strecker equilibrium and stabilizing the intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: N-formylation of aminonitriles in formamide has been shown to protect Strecker intermediates from reversion and thereby improve persistence of amino acid precursors in prebiotic systems (Green et al., 2021; Green et al., 2023). This is a literature-backed auxiliary branch that strengthens the main Strecker route without inventing a new core mechanism.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis and deprotection of aminonitrile pool to valine

conditions.other: prolonged aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal cycling; irreversible step drives Strecker network forward

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile hydrolysis proceeds through amide-level intermediates to the amino acid; N-formylated aminonitrile deprotects and hydrolyzes under extended aqueous cycling.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is the classical irreversible endpoint of Strecker synthesis and is specifically documented for valine precursors (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Devergne et al., 2021). Aminonitrile persistence and hydrolysis timescales under prebiotic conditions have been discussed by Miller and by later work on protected aminonitriles (Miller, 1998; Green et al., 2021; Green et al., 2023). Combining the unprotected and protected precursor pools here reflects convergent conversion to the same target.

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of isobutyraldehyde to valine hydantoin

conditions.other: carbonate/CO2-bearing cyanide-rich pool; mild evaporitic concentration

reactions.mechanism: A cyanide-, ammonia-, and CO2-assisted carbonyl capture pathway yields a hydantoin intermediate bridging Strecker-type aldehyde chemistry and alpha-keto-acid-style amino acid access.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs route is specifically identified in the literature as a variant pathway relevant to amino acid formation in the presence of carbonyl compounds, NH3, HCN, and CO2, connecting aldehyde chemistry with hydantoin intermediates (Pulletikurti et al., 2022). This is kept as an auxiliary branch from the same branched aldehyde bottleneck rather than an unrelated alternative.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine hydantoin to valine

conditions.other: extended aqueous hydrolysis under geothermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin intermediate releases valine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin intermediates are recognized prebiotic intermediates in Bucherer-Bergs-type amino acid synthesis and hydrolyze to amino acids under sustained aqueous conditions (Pulletikurti et al., 2022). This slower branch is retained because it is directly mentioned in the research report as a bridge between aldehyde chemistry and amino acid products.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal synthesis of pyruvate from reduced carbon feedstocks

conditions.other: moderate alkaline vent regime; anoxic; H2-rich serpentinization-fed system

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation and C-C bond formation in hydrothermal systems yields pyruvate as an alpha-keto-acid hub.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a plausible hydrothermal alpha-keto-acid hub in iron-sulfur/mineral catalysis frameworks and is consistent with vent-centered protometabolic chemistry. The reaction is assigned to moderate alkaline hydrothermal conditions rather than black-smoker extremes to respect amino acid stability constraints (Amend & Shock, 1998; Martin & Russell, 2007; Muchowska et al., 2019). The use of greigite/magnetite follows the required hydrothermal mineral framework and the provided task chemistry.

reactions.name: Speculative protometabolic branching from pyruvate and acetaldehyde to alpha-ketoisovalerate

conditions.other: protometabolic condensation network; likely via acetolactate-like intermediates not explicitly represented

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate and acetaldehyde feed a branching alpha-keto-acid network that can, in principle, furnish alpha-ketoisovalerate through multistep condensation/rearrangement chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies alpha-ketoisovalerate as a metabolically congruent precursor to valine but also emphasizes that its prebiotic synthesis remains experimentally unresolved. This reaction is therefore explicitly speculative and included only because the research report names condensation-based access from pyruvate-rich chemistry as a plausible alternative entry point (Copley et al., 2005; Muchowska et al., 2019; 2025 PMC report cited in the research summary). The omission of explicit acetolactate-like intermediates is a network simplification, and the speculative status is acknowledged.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.other: alkaline pH approximately 8-11; reducing hydrothermal microcompartments; FeS/H2S-like redox environment

reactions.mechanism: The branched alpha-keto acid undergoes mineral-mediated reductive amination with ammonia and reductant to give valine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids on iron-sulfur and related minerals is experimentally supported and metabolically congruent, though substrate-specific support for alpha-ketoisovalerate is more limited than for pyruvate. Relevant support includes FeS/H2S-pyrite formation systems, freshly precipitated FeS, mixed-valence iron minerals, and Ni-catalyzed reductive amination of biological keto acids (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Huber & Wachtershäuser, 2003; Barge et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024). This route is therefore plausible but less validated than the Strecker route, exactly as stated in the research findings.

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide coupling of valine to valylvaline

conditions.other: repeated wet-dry cycling in prebiotic pools; stoichiometrically represents self-condensation of two valine molecules

reactions.mechanism: Drying concentrates valine and drives ester-mediated or dehydration-assisted amide bond formation, yielding short oligopeptides such as valylvaline.
reactions.reasoning: Wet-dry cycling is a well-supported route to prebiotic peptide formation, and the reaction is included to show downstream biochemical incorporation of the target. The stoichiometry is intentionally noted as self-condensation of two valine molecules to avoid the unimolecular ambiguity criticized in prior attempts (Forsythe et al., 2015; Campbell et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical hydrothermal-to-surface Strecker route
pathways.description: Reduced carbon from hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry supplies acetaldehyde, which undergoes low-selectivity branched aldehyde diversification; the resulting isobutyraldehyde enters the validated Strecker sequence and hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: Protected Strecker route via N-formyl aminonitrile
pathways.description: The canonical Strecker aminonitrile is stabilized in formamide-rich surface settings by N-formylation before later hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin branch
pathways.description: The branched aldehyde precursor enters a CO2-assisted cyanide-ammonia hydantoin pathway and then hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: Surface-first Strecker from preconcentrated HCN pool
pathways.description: A surface HCN/formamide pool is established first; later delivery of hydrothermal acetaldehyde and low-yield branched aldehyde diversification complete the Strecker route.

pathways.name: Surface-first protected Strecker route
pathways.description: As in P4, but aminonitrile protection in formamide-rich drying films preserves the intermediate before hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Surface-first Bucherer-Bergs route
pathways.description: A cyanide- and CO2-containing surface pool receives branched aldehyde feed and channels it through a hydantoin intermediate to valine.

pathways.name: Protometabolic keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: Moderate hydrothermal carbon fixation yields pyruvate, which enters a speculative branching alpha-keto-acid network to alpha-ketoisovalerate, followed by mineral-mediated reductive amination to valine.

pathways.name: Mixed aldehyde and keto-acid convergence route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde feeds both the branched aldehyde Strecker branch and the alpha-ketoisovalerate branch, which converge independently on valine.

pathways.name: Hydantoin-to-valine-to-peptide route
pathways.description: Valine formed through the Bucherer-Bergs branch is subsequently incorporated into short peptides under wet-dry cycling.

pathways.name: Protected Strecker-to-peptide route
pathways.description: The protected aminonitrile branch supplies valine, which then undergoes prebiotic peptide coupling in concentrated drying pools.
